title: Welcome to the Age of Broke TV
excerpt: Amid a real-life economic downturn, the greatest shows of 2026—from “Beef” to “The Comeback”—are all about scraping by. Inkoo Kang writes.
content_html:
Soulless moguls and wealth porn are out. Amid a real-life economic downturn, the greatest shows of 2026—from “Beef” to “The Comeback”—are all about scraping by.

July 22, 2026

The FX drama “The Bear,” which began with the chef Carmy Berzatto’s dream of transforming his late brother’s sandwich shop into a fine-dining destination, has always been concerned with the precarity of the restaurant business. But in its final season, which aired last month, it dialled up the financial pressure as never before, leaving characters scrambling for solvency both in and out of the kitchen. They reduce portion sizes, retool menu items using cheaper substitutes, and commit to staying open despite a burst pipe, multiple equipment failures, and a massive storm, all on the same day. When the restaurant’s patron reveals a major downturn in his investment portfolio, the frenetic urgency that’s long defined the show is joined by the despondency of a sinking ship. One of the chefs overhears the sommelier begging for another job over the phone: “I’ll sweep up, work nights, whatever.”

When “The Bear” premièred, in 2022, it was something of an anomaly—a prestige series about the economy’s striving losers rather than its sore winners. Television has historically presented affluence as the norm, but recent shows like “Succession,” “The White Lotus,” and any number of “Big Little Lies” wannabes have taken the tendency further, trading the tasteful homes of the working rich for the frigid mansions of the generationally wealthy. For years, such shows effectively channelled both the populist anger and the wealth worship of the past two decades, inviting audiences to hate the élite while coveting their five-star amenities and their cashmere baseball caps. But jaundiced observations about the haves and the have-nots eventually reached a point of diminishing returns.

A fresh batch of shows has met the moment by focussing on the other side of the K-shaped economy: the downwardly mobile middle class. “DTF St. Louis,” “Beef,” and “The Comeback” feel tailor-made for an era marred by inflation anxiety, affordability crises, and fiscal doomerism; they’re also the best series of the year thus far. Where the seminal nineties sitcom “Roseanne” (still the gold standard for working-class stories onscreen) treated low expectations as both a way of life and a source of self-deprecating humor, these new programs capture the perspectives of those who fundamentally believe they should have more—and might in fact have more, in a fairer system. Though centered on society’s sinking middle, they spotlight an arguably universal experience: the emotional cost of living in a broken world.

The HBO drama “DTF St. Louis,” about the unusually tender bond between two suburban men, is particularly attuned to the way rocky finances can warp relationships. Clark Forrest is a local weatherman who becomes absorbed in the crises engulfing his new friend Floyd Smernitch, the station’s sign-language interpreter. Floyd is no longer attracted to his wife, has trouble connecting with his teen-age stepson, and owes tens of thousands in taxes—a set of stressors that are ruining his health. Even still, Clark is convinced that Floyd is extraordinary, comparing him to the sun. One night, the friends strip down to their underwear, dance side by side, and compliment each other on their bodies.

Hours later, Floyd turns up dead. “DTF St. Louis” unfurls as a murder mystery, but it’s most compelling as a portrait of a life in arrears. The Smernitches’ finances are so strapped that, when Floyd purchases a seventeen-dollar board game at a hobby store, he asks to spread the cost between two cards. He and his wife, Carol, are bombarded with calls and letters from creditors that remind them daily of their inability to claw their way up to zero. The possible seizure of their house hangs over them like a sword of Damocles. Floyd confides in Clark that his debts have left him with a fluttery “bird heart”; he’d love to have “a man heart again.” But with so little money to go around the amount that he owes keeps growing.

Naturally, his marriage takes a hit. After Carol, an accounting clerk, takes on a second job as an umpire, they stop having sex; he can’t stop picturing her in her bulky safety gear, a symbol of his inability to provide. Carol is alternately enchanted and frustrated by her husband’s innocence and altruism, which led him to his not particularly remunerative career as an interpreter. As much as she adores his softness, she has to harden herself to keep their household afloat. It’s no wonder that she pursues an affair with Clark, who passes himself off as a savvy entrepreneur during their initial encounter. And it doesn’t help that money would buy the Smernitches happiness, not least by getting Carol’s impulse-impaired son to a school that can meet his needs.

The erosion of romance by financial woes, a common plight in life, is surprisingly hard to find on TV—perhaps precisely because executives are skittish about hitting viewers too close to home. A scene in which Carol refuses Floyd’s gift of a pair of earrings, opting to spend the money on their bills instead, is especially brutal, and especially relatable. The show’s depiction of the suburbs—the stocky, beige-box houses; the stolen trysts in featureless motel rooms; the stultifying family time—feels refreshingly real. It’s also the diametric opposite of what many viewers seek from entertainment. Hence the popularity of a comparatively glossy series like “Margo’s Got Money Troubles,” about a college dropout and new mother who vanquishes all her worries with one weird trick: joining OnlyFans.

But “DTF St. Louis” proves hypnotic in its own way, as the Smernitches’ money troubles seep into husband and wife’s increasingly tangled relations with Clark. Carol persuades Clark to take out a life-insurance policy on her husband—an unusual request outside of a film noir, though not such a departure from the annals of heterosexuality, in which women have depended on men for security for millennia. Clark’s later offer to pay for their son’s private-school tuition is a more complicated largesse. Even for Floyd, who’s immune to so many traditional gender norms, the gift is an emasculating reminder of all he gave up when he ceased to be the breadwinner. On “DTF St. Louis,” emotions grow in unpredictable directions, but love seldom beats out shame.

The despair and helplessness are a lot less hushed on the second season of Netflix’s feud-based anthology series, “Beef.” A country-club manager and his wife are caught at home in the midst of a vicious argument by two members of his staff, who record the screaming match on a cellphone. A tug-of-war ensues over the footage, which the drudges see as leverage to secure better-paying jobs, and which their boss, Josh, fears is gnarly enough to get him fired if released. But yelling at his wife, Lindsay, is the least of his sins: to maintain their life style, Josh has been overdrawing an account that belonged to his dead mother, and he soon resorts to embezzlement. When he reveals the plot to Lindsay, she’s straightforwardly delighted. Her anger that Josh has left her too broke to even divorce him dissipates with his promise that they’ll “get back on track.”

The country club—a celebrity-studded establishment in Ojai—is an inspired setting for the season. It’s built on an upstairs-downstairs divide, but there’s no “White Lotus”-style churn of the guests; instead, the expectation of chummy vibes and the presence of regulars allow Josh and Lindsay to imagine themselves as the friends or peers of the patrons. While earlier class-conscious shows such as “The Wire” and “Maid” painstakingly explained institutional failures, “Beef” ’s character-driven approach illuminates a system’s flaws through the pathologies it produces. Josh, a perpetual self-optimizer, is convinced that an upswing is just around the corner. But his attempts to fraternize with club members have the opposite effect, as when he loses ten thousand dollars to Michael Phelps in a single round of poker. In the end, being around rich people costs Josh far more than it earns him.

Most of the American middle class isn’t hobnobbing with the likes of Phelps or Benny Blanco, but Josh’s risky wishful thinking—that some combination of “investing” in one’s network and spending one’s way to the top will inevitably pay off—is the kind that can take root more readily when no other paths are available. The delusion is painful to watch, but the greater tragedy is that his proximity to this staggering wealth insures his and Lindsay’s misery. The fact that their happiness is so dependent on success means they’ve practically set themselves up for marital collapse. Perennially being reminded of their place fuels the couple’s status anxiety, which in turn stirs up a tempest at home. Theirs is a relationship built on shared hopes for the future—and on the reflex to turn on each other when those dreams fail to materialize.

If “Beef” and “DTF” are focussed on the most intimate site of financial tension—marriage—“The Comeback” takes a more panoramic view. The third and final season of the HBO series opens with its heroine, an aging sitcom actress named Valerie Cherish, receiving an offer to star in a new multi-cam. This being “The Comeback,” a Hollywood satire whose protagonist pays for opportunities via novel forms of humiliation, there’s a very big catch: the multi-cam is to be written by artificial intelligence. As insulting as the role is, the rest of the industry doesn’t look much better: unemployment is rampant, and some of Valerie’s onetime colleagues have been forced to leave the business behind. (Jane, the reality-TV producer who trailed her in the first two seasons, is found working at Trader Joe’s.) For Valerie, who has downsized from a house in Brentwood to a condo in West Hollywood—and swapped her signature Birkin for a Tod’s tote—the choice is between working on an A.I. production and not working at all.

“The Comeback” dramatizes the more subtle human cost of tech-induced scarcity: not just the elimination of a vast sector of formerly reliable, union-strengthened jobs but the breakdown of vital collaborative structures and the sense of community that came with them. Not that the show is particularly nostalgic for the industry of yore, which was similarly rife with exploitation. No one is better versed in, say, the dysfunctions of the writer-actress relationship than Valerie, who, in previous seasons, suffered endlessly at the hands of a misogynistic showrunner. But, on her new sitcom, that relationship is severed altogether. The nominal showrunners have no incentive to punch up the lines that the A.I. churns out, which would be tantamount to training the machine designed to replace them. Neither they nor the A.I. are able to pull off the magic that successful multi-cams have relied on for decades: that of writers observing actors and playing to their strengths.

If A.I. discourse often revolves around jobs lost, “The Comeback” explores what it’s like to live and work amid the ensuing distrust, anger, and panic. Valerie is asked to speak out against her own show for the sake of human dignity—an act that seems noble on paper but risks putting two hundred or so of her crew members out on the street. When they discover that they’ve been duped into working on an A.I. production, morale plummets. They’re not the only ones to fall victim to a contractual bait and switch; in the end, the streamer replaces both Valerie and her castmates with digital scans of themselves.

“The Comeback” is an insider’s parody: the show is co-created by and stars the “Friends” alum Lisa Kudrow, who’s intimately familiar with the arduous work of making people laugh, and with the way an actor’s career can wax and wane over time. But this season feels much more expansive than its predecessors, warning of the personal and professional diminishment that may lie ahead in the increasingly insecure future that tech moguls have promised. Valerie, the consummate survivor, will doubtless find a way to keep her head above water, even if Hollywood goes down in an A.I.-pocalypse. The pessimism that tugs at the rest of us is best encapsulated by the contraceptive pills that Josh’s wife, Lindsay, takes with resigned practicality on “Beef.” The couple have had numerous conversations about starting a family, but, like many real-life millennials, they’ve responded to their circumstances by hedging their bets. There never seems to be a “right time” in an age of decline. ♦

Inkoo Kang, a staff writer, has been a television critic for The New Yorker since 2022.

sentences.zh: 多年来，此类节目有效地传达了过去二十年的民粹主义愤怒和财富崇拜，让观众憎恨精英，同时觊觎他们的五星级设施和羊绒棒球帽。

sentences.en: But jaundiced observations about the haves and the have-nots eventually reached a point of diminishing returns.
sentences.zh: 但对富人和穷人的偏见观察最终达到了收益递减的地步。

blocks.html_en: A fresh batch of shows has met the moment by focussing on the other side of the K-shaped economy: the downwardly mobile middle class. “DTF St. Louis,” “Beef,” and “The Comeback” feel tailor-made for an era marred by inflation anxiety, affordability crises, and fiscal doomerism; they’re also the best series of the year thus far. Where the seminal nineties sitcom “Roseanne” (still the gold standard for working-class stories onscreen) treated low expectations as both a way of life and a source of self-deprecating humor, these new programs capture the perspectives of those who fundamentally believe they should have more—and might in fact have more, in a fairer system. Though centered on society’s sinking middle, they spotlight an arguably universal experience: the emotional cost of living in a broken world.

sentences.en: A fresh batch of shows has met the moment by focussing on the other side of the K-shaped economy: the downwardly mobile middle class.
sentences.zh: 一批新的节目通过关注 K 型经济的另一面：向下流动的中产阶级来迎接这一时刻。

sentences.en: “DTF St. Louis,” “Beef,” and “The Comeback” feel tailor-made for an era marred by inflation anxiety, affordability crises, and fiscal doomerism; they’re also the best series of the year thus far.
sentences.zh: 《DTF 圣路易斯》、《牛肉》和《卷土重来》感觉是为一个饱受通胀焦虑、负担能力危机和财政悲观主义困扰的时代量身定做的；它们也是迄今为止今年最好的系列。

sentences.en: Where the seminal nineties sitcom “Roseanne” (still the gold standard for working-class stories onscreen) treated low expectations as both a way of life and a source of self-deprecating humor, these new programs capture the perspectives of those who fundamentally believe they should have more—and might in fact have more, in a fairer system.
sentences.zh: 九十年代开创性的情景喜剧《Roseanne》（至今仍是银幕工人阶级故事的黄金标准）将低期望视为一种生活方式和自嘲式幽默的来源，而这些新节目抓住了那些从根本上相信自己应该拥有更多的人的观点——而且在一个更公平的制度下实际上可能拥有更多。

sentences.en: Though centered on society’s sinking middle, they spotlight an arguably universal experience: the emotional cost of living in a broken world.
sentences.zh: 虽然以社会下沉的中层为中心，但它们强调了一种可以说是普遍的经历：生活在破碎世界中的情感成本。

blocks.html_en: The HBO drama “DTF St. Louis,” about the unusually tender bond between two suburban men, is particularly attuned to the way rocky finances can warp relationships. Clark Forrest is a local weatherman who becomes absorbed in the crises engulfing his new friend Floyd Smernitch, the station’s sign-language interpreter. Floyd is no longer attracted to his wife, has trouble connecting with his teen-age stepson, and owes tens of thousands in taxes—a set of stressors that are ruining his health. Even still, Clark is convinced that Floyd is extraordinary, comparing him to the sun. One night, the friends strip down to their underwear, dance side by side, and compliment each other on their bodies.

sentences.en: The HBO drama “DTF St. Louis,” about the unusually tender bond between two suburban men, is particularly attuned to the way rocky finances can warp relationships.
sentences.zh: HBO 电视剧《DTF 圣路易斯》讲述了两个郊区男人之间异常温柔的关系，尤其反映了不稳定的财务状况如何扭曲人际关系。

sentences.en: Clark Forrest is a local weatherman who becomes absorbed in the crises engulfing his new friend Floyd Smernitch, the station’s sign-language interpreter.
sentences.zh: 克拉克·福雷斯特 (Clark Forrest) 是当地的一名气象员，他全神贯注于席卷他的新朋友、电台手语翻译弗洛伊德·斯默尼奇 (Floyd Smernitch) 的危机。

sentences.en: Floyd is no longer attracted to his wife, has trouble connecting with his teen-age stepson, and owes tens of thousands in taxes—a set of stressors that are ruining his health.
sentences.zh: 弗洛伊德不再被他的妻子所吸引，与他十几岁的继子难以联系，并且欠了数万美元的税款——一系列的压力正在损害他的健康。

sentences.en: Even still, Clark is convinced that Floyd is extraordinary, comparing him to the sun.
sentences.zh: 尽管如此，克拉克仍然坚信弗洛伊德是非凡的，并将他比作太阳。

sentences.en: One night, the friends strip down to their underwear, dance side by side, and compliment each other on their bodies.
sentences.zh: 一天晚上，朋友们脱光内衣，并肩跳舞，互相称赞对方的身材。

blocks.html_en: Hours later, Floyd turns up dead. “DTF St. Louis” unfurls as a murder mystery, but it’s most compelling as a portrait of a life in arrears. The Smernitches’ finances are so strapped that, when Floyd purchases a seventeen-dollar board game at a hobby store, he asks to spread the cost between two cards. He and his wife, Carol, are bombarded with calls and letters from creditors that remind them daily of their inability to claw their way up to zero. The possible seizure of their house hangs over them like a sword of Damocles. Floyd confides in Clark that his debts have left him with a fluttery “bird heart”; he’d love to have “a man heart again.” But with so little money to go around the amount that he owes keeps growing.

sentences.en: Hours later, Floyd turns up dead.
sentences.zh: 几个小时后，弗洛伊德被发现死亡。

sentences.en: “DTF St. Louis” unfurls as a murder mystery, but it’s most compelling as a portrait of a life in arrears.
sentences.zh: 《DTF 圣路易斯》以谋杀之谜的形式展开，但最引人注目的是对拖欠生活的描绘。

sentences.en: The Smernitches’ finances are so strapped that, when Floyd purchases a seventeen-dollar board game at a hobby store, he asks to spread the cost between two cards.
sentences.zh: Smernitches 的财务状况非常紧张，以至于当弗洛伊德在一家业余爱好商店购买了一款 17 美元的棋盘游戏时，他要求将费用分摊到两张卡上。

sentences.zh: 他和他的妻子卡罗尔每天都受到债权人的电话和信件的轰炸，这些债权人每天都在提醒他们，他们无法努力实现零利率。

sentences.zh: 他们的房子可能被没收，就像一把达摩克利斯之剑悬在他们的头顶。

sentences.en: Floyd confides in Clark that his debts have left him with a fluttery “bird heart”; he’d love to have “a man heart again.”
sentences.zh: 弗洛伊德向克拉克吐露，他的债务让他拥有了一颗躁动的“鸟心”；他很想“再次拥有一颗男人心”。

sentences.zh: 但由于可用资金有限，他所欠的金额却不断增加。

blocks.html_en: Naturally, his marriage takes a hit. After Carol, an accounting clerk, takes on a second job as an umpire, they stop having sex; he can’t stop picturing her in her bulky safety gear, a symbol of his inability to provide. Carol is alternately enchanted and frustrated by her husband’s innocence and altruism, which led him to his not particularly remunerative career as an interpreter. As much as she adores his softness, she has to harden herself to keep their household afloat. It’s no wonder that she pursues an affair with Clark, who passes himself off as a savvy entrepreneur during their initial encounter. And it doesn’t help that money would buy the Smernitches happiness, not least by getting Carol’s impulse-impaired son to a school that can meet his needs.

sentences.zh: 自然，他的婚姻受到了打击。

sentences.en: After Carol, an accounting clerk, takes on a second job as an umpire, they stop having sex; he can’t stop picturing her in her bulky safety gear, a symbol of his inability to provide.

blocks.html_en: The erosion of romance by financial woes, a common plight in life, is surprisingly hard to find on TV—perhaps precisely because executives are skittish about hitting viewers too close to home. A scene in which Carol refuses Floyd’s gift of a pair of earrings, opting to spend the money on their bills instead, is especially brutal, and especially relatable. The show’s depiction of the suburbs—the stocky, beige-box houses; the stolen trysts in featureless motel rooms; the stultifying family time—feels refreshingly real. It’s also the diametric opposite of what many viewers seek from entertainment. Hence the popularity of a comparatively glossy series like “Margo’s Got Money Troubles,” about a college dropout and new mother who vanquishes all her worries with one weird trick: joining OnlyFans.

sentences.en: The erosion of romance by financial woes, a common plight in life, is surprisingly hard to find on TV—perhaps precisely because executives are skittish about hitting viewers too close to home.

sentences.en: Hence the popularity of a comparatively glossy series like “Margo’s Got Money Troubles,” about a college dropout and new mother who vanquishes all her worries with one weird trick: joining OnlyFans.
sentences.zh: 因此，像《玛戈的钱有问题》这样相对光鲜亮丽的连续剧大受欢迎，该剧讲述了一位大学辍学生和新妈妈用一个奇怪的技巧克服了所有的担忧：加入 OnlyFans。

blocks.html_en: But “DTF St. Louis” proves hypnotic in its own way, as the Smernitches’ money troubles seep into husband and wife’s increasingly tangled relations with Clark. Carol persuades Clark to take out a life-insurance policy on her husband—an unusual request outside of a film noir, though not such a departure from the annals of heterosexuality, in which women have depended on men for security for millennia. Clark’s later offer to pay for their son’s private-school tuition is a more complicated largesse. Even for Floyd, who’s immune to so many traditional gender norms, the gift is an emasculating reminder of all he gave up when he ceased to be the breadwinner. On “DTF St. Louis,” emotions grow in unpredictable directions, but love seldom beats out shame.

sentences.en: But “DTF St. Louis” proves hypnotic in its own way, as the Smernitches’ money troubles seep into husband and wife’s increasingly tangled relations with Clark.

blocks.html_en: The two couples at the heart of the new season of “Beef” are both teetering on the edge of advancement—or ruin.Photograph courtesy Netflix

sentences.en: The two couples at the heart of the new season of “Beef” are both teetering on the edge of advancement—or ruin.Photograph courtesy Netflix
sentences.zh: 新一季《牛肉》的核心人物两对夫妇都在进步或毁灭的边缘摇摇欲坠。照片由 Netflix 提供

blocks.html_en: The despair and helplessness are a lot less hushed on the second season of Netflix’s feud-based anthology series, “Beef.” A country-club manager and his wife are caught at home in the midst of a vicious argument by two members of his staff, who record the screaming match on a cellphone. A tug-of-war ensues over the footage, which the drudges see as leverage to secure better-paying jobs, and which their boss, Josh, fears is gnarly enough to get him fired if released. But yelling at his wife, Lindsay, is the least of his sins: to maintain their life style, Josh has been overdrawing an account that belonged to his dead mother, and he soon resorts to embezzlement. When he reveals the plot to Lindsay, she’s straightforwardly delighted. Her anger that Josh has left her too broke to even divorce him dissipates with his promise that they’ll “get back on track.”

sentences.en: The despair and helplessness are a lot less hushed on the second season of Netflix’s feud-based anthology series, “Beef.”
sentences.zh: Netflix 以争斗为主题的选集系列剧《牛肉》第二季中的绝望和无助不再那么平静。

sentences.en: A country-club manager and his wife are caught at home in the midst of a vicious argument by two members of his staff, who record the screaming match on a cellphone.

sentences.zh: 围绕这段视频展开了一场拉锯战，苦工们认为这些视频是获得高薪工作的杠杆，而他们的老板乔什担心，如果发布这些视频，他可能会被解雇。

sentences.en: But yelling at his wife, Lindsay, is the least of his sins: to maintain their life style, Josh has been overdrawing an account that belonged to his dead mother, and he soon resorts to embezzlement.
sentences.zh: 但对他的妻子林赛大喊大叫是他犯下的最小的罪过：为了维持他们的生活方式，乔什一直在透支属于他已故母亲的账户，他很快就采取了贪污的行为。

sentences.zh: 当他向林赛透露这个情节时，她非常高兴。

sentences.zh: 她对乔什让她破产，甚至无法与他离婚的愤怒随着他承诺他们会“重回正轨”而消散。

blocks.html_en: The country club—a celebrity-studded establishment in Ojai—is an inspired setting for the season. It’s built on an upstairs-downstairs divide, but there’s no “White Lotus”-style churn of the guests; instead, the expectation of chummy vibes and the presence of regulars allow Josh and Lindsay to imagine themselves as the friends or peers of the patrons. While earlier class-conscious shows such as “The Wire” and “Maid” painstakingly explained institutional failures, “Beef” ’s character-driven approach illuminates a system’s flaws through the pathologies it produces. Josh, a perpetual self-optimizer, is convinced that an upswing is just around the corner. But his attempts to fraternize with club members have the opposite effect, as when he loses ten thousand dollars to Michael Phelps in a single round of poker. In the end, being around rich people costs Josh far more than it earns him.

sentences.en: The country club—a celebrity-studded establishment in Ojai—is an inspired setting for the season.
sentences.zh: 位于奥海 (Ojai) 的乡村俱乐部是名人云集的场所，是这个季节充满灵感的场所。

sentences.en: It’s built on an upstairs-downstairs divide, but there’s no “White Lotus”-style churn of the guests; instead, the expectation of chummy vibes and the presence of regulars allow Josh and Lindsay to imagine themselves as the friends or peers of the patrons.
sentences.zh: 它建在楼上楼下的分界线上，但没有“白莲花”式的客人流失；相反，对亲密氛围的期望和常客的存在让乔什和林赛将自己想象成顾客的朋友或同龄人。

sentences.en: While earlier class-conscious shows such as “The Wire” and “Maid” painstakingly explained institutional failures, “Beef” ’s character-driven approach illuminates a system’s flaws through the pathologies it produces.
sentences.zh: 虽然《火线》和《女仆》等早期的阶级意识剧煞费苦心地解释了制度的失败，但《牛肉》的角色驱动方法通过系统产生的病态揭示了系统的缺陷。

sentences.en: Josh, a perpetual self-optimizer, is convinced that an upswing is just around the corner.
sentences.zh: 乔什是一个永远自我优化的人，他坚信上升指日可待。

sentences.en: But his attempts to fraternize with club members have the opposite effect, as when he loses ten thousand dollars to Michael Phelps in a single round of poker.
sentences.zh: 但他与俱乐部成员友好相处的尝试却产生了相反的效果，比如他在一轮扑克游戏中输给了迈克尔·菲尔普斯 (Michael Phelps) 一万美元。

sentences.en: In the end, being around rich people costs Josh far more than it earns him.
sentences.zh: 最终，乔什与有钱人相处所付出的代价远远超过了他所获得的收入。

blocks.html_en: Most of the American middle class isn’t hobnobbing with the likes of Phelps or Benny Blanco, but Josh’s risky wishful thinking—that some combination of “investing” in one’s network and spending one’s way to the top will inevitably pay off—is the kind that can take root more readily when no other paths are available. The delusion is painful to watch, but the greater tragedy is that his proximity to this staggering wealth insures his and Lindsay’s misery. The fact that their happiness is so dependent on success means they’ve practically set themselves up for marital collapse. Perennially being reminded of their place fuels the couple’s status anxiety, which in turn stirs up a tempest at home. Theirs is a relationship built on shared hopes for the future—and on the reflex to turn on each other when those dreams fail to materialize.

sentences.en: Most of the American middle class isn’t hobnobbing with the likes of Phelps or Benny Blanco, but Josh’s risky wishful thinking—that some combination of “investing” in one’s network and spending one’s way to the top will inevitably pay off—is the kind that can take root more readily when no other paths are available.

blocks.html_en: If “Beef” and “DTF” are focussed on the most intimate site of financial tension—marriage—“The Comeback” takes a more panoramic view. The third and final season of the HBO series opens with its heroine, an aging sitcom actress named Valerie Cherish, receiving an offer to star in a new multi-cam. This being “The Comeback,” a Hollywood satire whose protagonist pays for opportunities via novel forms of humiliation, there’s a very big catch: the multi-cam is to be written by artificial intelligence. As insulting as the role is, the rest of the industry doesn’t look much better: unemployment is rampant, and some of Valerie’s onetime colleagues have been forced to leave the business behind. (Jane, the reality-TV producer who trailed her in the first two seasons, is found working at Trader Joe’s.) For Valerie, who has downsized from a house in Brentwood to a condo in West Hollywood—and swapped her signature Birkin for a Tod’s tote—the choice is between working on an A.I. production and not working at all.

blocks.html_en: “The Comeback” dramatizes the more subtle human cost of tech-induced scarcity: not just the elimination of a vast sector of formerly reliable, union-strengthened jobs but the breakdown of vital collaborative structures and the sense of community that came with them. Not that the show is particularly nostalgic for the industry of yore, which was similarly rife with exploitation. No one is better versed in, say, the dysfunctions of the writer-actress relationship than Valerie, who, in previous seasons, suffered endlessly at the hands of a misogynistic showrunner. But, on her new sitcom, that relationship is severed altogether. The nominal showrunners have no incentive to punch up the lines that the A.I. churns out, which would be tantamount to training the machine designed to replace them. Neither they nor the A.I. are able to pull off the magic that successful multi-cams have relied on for decades: that of writers observing actors and playing to their strengths.

blocks.html_en: If A.I. discourse often revolves around jobs lost, “The Comeback” explores what it’s like to live and work amid the ensuing distrust, anger, and panic. Valerie is asked to speak out against her own show for the sake of human dignity—an act that seems noble on paper but risks putting two hundred or so of her crew members out on the street. When they discover that they’ve been duped into working on an A.I. production, morale plummets. They’re not the only ones to fall victim to a contractual bait and switch; in the end, the streamer replaces both Valerie and her castmates with digital scans of themselves.

blocks.html_en: “The Comeback” is an insider’s parody: the show is co-created by and stars the “Friends” alum Lisa Kudrow, who’s intimately familiar with the arduous work of making people laugh, and with the way an actor’s career can wax and wane over time. But this season feels much more expansive than its predecessors, warning of the personal and professional diminishment that may lie ahead in the increasingly insecure future that tech moguls have promised. Valerie, the consummate survivor, will doubtless find a way to keep her head above water, even if Hollywood goes down in an A.I.-pocalypse. The pessimism that tugs at the rest of us is best encapsulated by the contraceptive pills that Josh’s wife, Lindsay, takes with resigned practicality on “Beef.” The couple have had numerous conversations about starting a family, but, like many real-life millennials, they’ve responded to their circumstances by hedging their bets. There never seems to be a “right time” in an age of decline. ♦

content_html_zh:
没有灵魂的大亨和财富色情片已经过时了。 在现实经济低迷的情况下，2026 年最伟大的剧集——从《牛肉》到《回归》——都是在勉强度日。

July 22, 2026

FX 电视剧《熊》以主厨卡米·贝尔扎托 (Carmy Berzatto) 梦想将其已故兄弟的三明治店改造成美食胜地开始，一直关注餐饮业的不稳定状况。 但在上个月播出的最后一季中，它前所未有地承受了财务压力，让角色们在厨房内外都在争夺偿付能力。 他们减少份量，使用更便宜的替代品重新调整菜单项，并承诺在同一天尽管发生管道爆裂、多台设备故障和大风暴，仍保持营业。 当餐厅的顾客透露他的投资组合大幅下滑时，长期以来定义该节目的疯狂紧迫感与一艘正在下沉的船的沮丧交织在一起。 一位厨师无意中听到侍酒师在电话里乞求另一份工作：“我会打扫卫生，晚上工作，等等。”

当《熊》于 2022 年首映时，它在某种程度上是一种反常现象——一部讲述经济中挣扎的失败者而不是痛苦的赢家的声望剧集。 电视历史上一直将富裕视为常态，但最近的电视剧，如《继承之战》、《白莲花》，以及许多追随《大小谎言》的人，进一步推动了这一趋势，将工薪阶层的雅致住宅换成了一代富人的冰冷豪宅。 多年来，此类节目有效地传达了过去二十年的民粹主义愤怒和财富崇拜，让观众憎恨精英，同时觊觎他们的五星级设施和羊绒棒球帽。 但对富人和穷人的偏见观察最终达到了收益递减的地步。

一批新的节目通过关注 K 型经济的另一面：向下流动的中产阶级来迎接这一时刻。 《DTF 圣路易斯》、《牛肉》和《卷土重来》感觉是为一个饱受通胀焦虑、负担能力危机和财政悲观主义困扰的时代量身定做的；它们也是迄今为止今年最好的系列。 九十年代开创性的情景喜剧《Roseanne》（至今仍是银幕工人阶级故事的黄金标准）将低期望视为一种生活方式和自嘲式幽默的来源，而这些新节目抓住了那些从根本上相信自己应该拥有更多的人的观点——而且在一个更公平的制度下实际上可能拥有更多。 虽然以社会下沉的中层为中心，但它们强调了一种可以说是普遍的经历：生活在破碎世界中的情感成本。

HBO 电视剧《DTF 圣路易斯》讲述了两个郊区男人之间异常温柔的关系，尤其反映了不稳定的财务状况如何扭曲人际关系。 克拉克·福雷斯特 (Clark Forrest) 是当地的一名气象员，他全神贯注于席卷他的新朋友、电台手语翻译弗洛伊德·斯默尼奇 (Floyd Smernitch) 的危机。 弗洛伊德不再被他的妻子所吸引，与他十几岁的继子难以联系，并且欠了数万美元的税款——一系列的压力正在损害他的健康。 尽管如此，克拉克仍然坚信弗洛伊德是非凡的，并将他比作太阳。 一天晚上，朋友们脱光内衣，并肩跳舞，互相称赞对方的身材。

几个小时后，弗洛伊德被发现死亡。 《DTF 圣路易斯》以谋杀之谜的形式展开，但最引人注目的是对拖欠生活的描绘。 Smernitches 的财务状况非常紧张，以至于当弗洛伊德在一家业余爱好商店购买了一款 17 美元的棋盘游戏时，他要求将费用分摊到两张卡上。 他和他的妻子卡罗尔每天都受到债权人的电话和信件的轰炸，这些债权人每天都在提醒他们，他们无法努力实现零利率。 他们的房子可能被没收，就像一把达摩克利斯之剑悬在他们的头顶。 弗洛伊德向克拉克吐露，他的债务让他拥有了一颗躁动的“鸟心”；他很想“再次拥有一颗男人心”。 但由于可用资金有限，他所欠的金额却不断增加。

自然，他的婚姻受到了打击。 会计职员卡罗尔担任第二份裁判工作后，他们不再发生性行为；他无法停止想象她穿着笨重的安全装备，这是他无能为力的象征。 卡罗尔对丈夫的纯真和利他主义时而着迷，时而沮丧，这导致他从事了一份报酬并不特别丰厚的翻译职业。 尽管她很喜欢他的温柔，但她必须坚强自己才能维持家庭的生计。 难怪她与克拉克有染，克拉克在他们初次见面时假装自己是一位精明的企业家。 金钱买来斯默尼奇一家的幸福也无济于事，尤其是让卡罗尔冲动受损的儿子去一所能够满足他需求的学校。

经济困境对浪漫的侵蚀是生活中常见的困境，但令人惊讶的是，在电视上很难看到这种情况——也许正是因为高管们对影响离家太近的观众感到不安。 卡罗尔拒绝了弗洛伊德赠送的一对耳环，而是选择把钱花在账单上，这一场景尤其残酷，也特别能引起共鸣。 该剧对郊区的描绘——矮胖的米色盒子房子；在毫无特色的汽车旅馆房间里偷来的幽会；沉闷的家庭时光——感觉真实得令人耳目一新。 这也与许多观众寻求的娱乐截然相反。 因此，像《玛戈的钱有问题》这样相对光鲜亮丽的连续剧大受欢迎，该剧讲述了一位大学辍学生和新妈妈用一个奇怪的技巧克服了所有的担忧：加入 OnlyFans。

但《DTF 圣路易斯》以自己的方式证明了它的催眠作用，因为斯默尼奇夫妇的金钱问题渗透到了夫妻俩与克拉克之间日益复杂的关系中。 卡罗尔说服克拉克为她的丈夫购买一份人寿保险——这在黑色电影之外是一个不寻常的要求，尽管这并没有背离异性恋的历史，在异性恋中，女性几千年来一直依赖男性来获得安全感。 克拉克后来提出支付儿子的私立学校学费，这是一笔更为复杂的慷慨。 即使对于不受如此多传统性别规范影响的弗洛伊德来说，这份礼物也是一种阉割式的提醒，让他想起当他不再是养家糊口的人时他所放弃的一切。 在《DTF St. Louis》中，情感朝着不可预测的方向发展，但爱很少能战胜羞耻。

Netflix 以争斗为主题的选集系列剧《牛肉》第二季中的绝望和无助不再那么平静。 一位乡村俱乐部经理和他的妻子在家中被两名员工发现正在激烈争吵，他们用手机记录了这场尖叫的比赛。 围绕这段视频展开了一场拉锯战，苦工们认为这些视频是获得高薪工作的杠杆，而他们的老板乔什担心，如果发布这些视频，他可能会被解雇。 但对他的妻子林赛大喊大叫是他犯下的最小的罪过：为了维持他们的生活方式，乔什一直在透支属于他已故母亲的账户，他很快就采取了贪污的行为。 当他向林赛透露这个情节时，她非常高兴。 她对乔什让她破产，甚至无法与他离婚的愤怒随着他承诺他们会“重回正轨”而消散。

位于奥海 (Ojai) 的乡村俱乐部是名人云集的场所，是这个季节充满灵感的场所。 它建在楼上楼下的分界线上，但没有“白莲花”式的客人流失；相反，对亲密氛围的期望和常客的存在让乔什和林赛将自己想象成顾客的朋友或同龄人。 虽然《火线》和《女仆》等早期的阶级意识剧煞费苦心地解释了制度的失败，但《牛肉》的角色驱动方法通过系统产生的病态揭示了系统的缺陷。 乔什是一个永远自我优化的人，他坚信上升指日可待。 但他与俱乐部成员友好相处的尝试却产生了相反的效果，比如他在一轮扑克游戏中输给了迈克尔·菲尔普斯 (Michael Phelps) 一万美元。 最终，乔什与有钱人相处所付出的代价远远超过了他所获得的收入。

大多数美国中产阶级并不与菲尔普斯或本尼·布兰科这样的人关系密切，但乔什的冒险一厢情愿——“投资”一个人的网络和花钱登上顶峰的某种组合将不可避免地得到回报——是一种在没有其他路径可用的情况下更容易扎根的想法。 这种错觉看起来很痛苦，但更大的悲剧是，他与这笔惊人财富的接近确保了他和林赛的痛苦。 事实上，他们的幸福如此依赖于成功，这意味着他们实际上已经为婚姻的崩溃做好了准备。 不断地提醒他们自己的位置加剧了这对夫妇的地位焦虑，这反过来又在家里激起了一场风暴。 他们的关系建立在对未来的共同希望之上，并且当这些梦想未能实现时，他们会本能地互相攻击。

如果说《牛肉》和《DTF》聚焦的是财务紧张最亲密的场所——婚姻，那么《回归》则采取了更为全景的视角。 HBO 剧集第三季也是最后一季以女主人公、一位名叫瓦莱丽·切里什 (Valerie Cherish) 的老年情景喜剧女演员开始，她收到了出演一部新的多镜头影片的邀请。 这是一部好莱坞讽刺剧《卷土重来》，主角通过新颖的羞辱形式来换取机会，但有一个很大的问题：多镜头将由人工智能编写。 尽管这个角色很侮辱人，但该行业的其他公司看起来也好不到哪里去：失业率很高，瓦莱丽的一些前同事被迫离开了公司。 （在前两季中跟踪她的真人秀制片人简被发现在 Trader Joe's 工作。） 瓦莱丽 (Valerie) 从布伦特伍德 (Brentwood) 的房子搬到了西好莱坞 (West Hollywood) 的一套公寓，并把她标志性的柏金包 (Birkin) 换成了托德 (Tod’s) 手提包，对于她来说，她面临的选择是，要么为人工智能工作，要么为人工智能工作。 生产并且根本不工作。

《卷土重来》戏剧化地描绘了技术导致的稀缺性所造成的更微妙的人力成本：不仅消除了大量以前可靠的、工会加强的工作岗位，而且还破坏了重要的协作结构以及随之而来的社区意识。 这并不是说这部剧特别怀念昔日的行业，因为过去的行业同样充斥着剥削。 没有人比瓦莱丽更了解编剧与演员关系的失调，她在前几季中，在一位厌恶女性的制片人的手中遭受了无尽的痛苦。 但是，在她的新情景喜剧中，这种关系完全断绝了。 名义上的制片人没有动力去强调人工智能的台词。 大量生产，这相当于训练旨在取代它们的机器。 他们和人工智能都没有。 他们能够实现几十年来成功的多镜头所依赖的魔力：编剧观察演员并发挥他们的优势。

如果人工智能 讨论常常围绕着失去的工作展开，《卷土重来》探讨了在随之而来的不信任、愤怒和恐慌中生活和工作的感觉。 瓦莱丽被要求为了人的尊严而公开反对她自己的节目——这种行为在纸面上看似高尚，但有可能让大约 200 名工作人员流落街头。 当他们发现自己被骗去开发人工智能时。 生产下降，士气低落。 他们并不是唯一成为合同诱饵和转换受害者的人；最后，主播用他们自己的数字扫描代替了瓦莱丽和她的剧组成员。

《卷土重来》是对业内人士的戏仿：该剧由曾出演《老友记》的丽莎·库卓共同创作并主演，她非常熟悉让人们开怀大笑的艰巨工作，以及演员的职业生涯随着时间的推移而起起落落的方式。 但这一季给人的感觉比前几季要广阔得多，警告人们在科技巨头所承诺的日益不安全的未来中，个人和职业可能会受到削弱。 瓦莱丽，这位完美的幸存者，无疑会找到一种方法让自己保持在水面上，即使好莱坞在人工智能大灾难中陷入困境。 乔什的妻子林赛在《牛肉》中以一种听天由命的实际态度服用了避孕药，这最能体现我们其他人的悲观情绪。 这对夫妇就组建家庭进行了多次对话，但像许多现实生活中的千禧一代一样，他们通过两面下注来应对自己的处境。 在衰落的时代似乎从来没有“正确的时机”。 ❖

Inkoo Kang 是一名特约撰稿人，自 2022 年以来一直担任《纽约客》的电视评论家。
